questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted memory within allocated partition (e.g., 64KB partition for 50KB process = 14KB wasted)
External fragmentation: total free memory exists but not contiguous (e.g., scattered 10KB, 20KB, 15KB holes cannot fit 40KB process)
Internal occurs in fixed partitioning; External occurs in variable partitioning

questions.question: Describe the three contiguous memory allocation strategies: First Fit, Best Fit, and Worst Fit.
keyPoints: First Fit: scan from beginning, allocate first hole large enough - fastest
Best Fit: allocate smallest hole large enough - minimizes leftover fragment but slower
Worst Fit: allocate largest hole - creates largest leftover fragment

questions.question: What is memory compaction and how does it help in memory management?
keyPoints: Compaction moves all free memory holes together to form one contiguous block
Helps combat external fragmentation by consolidating scattered free memory
Requires relocation of all processes (address binding at execution time)
Time-consuming operation that interrupts system operation

questions.question: Explain how base and limit registers provide memory protection in contiguous allocation.
keyPoints: Base register: holds starting physical address of process partition
Limit register: holds size/length of the allocated partition
CPU generates logical address: valid if offset < limit register value
Physical address = base register + offset
Any access outside this range generates a trap/protection fault

questions.question: Differentiate between fixed partitioning and variable partitioning in memory management.
keyPoints: Fixed: memory divided into predetermined partitions at system startup
Variable: partitions created dynamically based on process size
Fixed has internal fragmentation, variable has external fragmentation
Variable allows more efficient memory utilization
Fixed limits degree of multiprogramming to number of partitions

questions.question: What is swapping in the context of contiguous memory allocation?
questions.back: Define swapping and explain its role
keyPoints: Swapping: moving entire processes in and out of main memory
Process can be temporarily moved to secondary storage (swap space)
Makes room for other processes in memory
When process completes I/O, it is swapped back to memory
Allows execution of processes larger than physical memory
In contiguous allocation, entire process must be swapped as a unit

questions.question: Explain the concept of address binding and its three types in operating systems.
keyPoints: Address binding: mapping symbolic/logical addresses to physical addresses
Compile time: physical addresses known at compile time (no relocation)
Load time: binding done when program is loaded into memory
Execution time: binding done during execution (allows process movement)
Contiguous allocation typically uses execution time binding for compaction support

questions.question: Consider a memory with holes of sizes: 300KB, 200KB, 500KB, 150KB, and 400KB (in that order). Show how each of the following allocation strategies would allocate processes of sizes: 210KB, 180KB, 320KB, and 100KB. Calculate the internal/external fragmentation if applicable.
keyPoints: First Fit: 300KB→210KB leaves 90KB hole, 200KB→180KB leaves 20KB, 500KB→320KB leaves 180KB, 150KB→100KB leaves 50KB
Best Fit: 200KB→180KB (smallest), 300KB→210KB (smallest), 400KB→320KB (smallest), 150KB→100KB (smallest)
Worst Fit: 500KB→320KB leaves 180KB, 500KB→210KB leaves 290KB (new order), 300KB→180KB, 150KB→100KB
Fixed partitioning has internal fragmentation; Variable partitioning has external fragmentation
Track remaining holes after each allocation

questions.question: A computer system has memory of 1MB. Fixed partitioning is used with four partitions of sizes 256KB, 256KB, 256KB, and 256KB. Consider the following sequence of processes: P1 (150KB), P2 (300KB), P3 (100KB), P4 (200KB). Show how these processes would be loaded and calculate total internal fragmentation.
keyPoints: P1(150KB)→Partition1: internal frag=256-150=106KB
P2(300KB) cannot fit in any 256KB partition - REJECTED
P3(100KB)→Partition2: internal frag=256-100=156KB
P4(200KB)→Partition3: internal frag=256-200=56KB
One partition remains empty (256KB)
Total internal fragmentation = 106+156+56 = 318KB
Only 3 processes loaded due to partition size limitation
Shows limitation of fixed partitioning: degree of multiprogramming = 4

questions.question: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of contiguous memory allocation. Also discuss how the disadvantages can be mitigated.
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to implement, fast memory access (contiguous addresses), low overhead, easy memory protection using base/limit registers
Disadvantages: External fragmentation (variable partitioning), Internal fragmentation (fixed partitioning), Limited degree of multiprogramming (fixed), Processes must fit contiguously
Mitigations: Compaction for external fragmentation, Unequal partitions for reduced internal fragmentation, Paging/segmentation as alternatives
Compaction: move all processes to consolidate free memory (time-consuming)
Choice depends on system requirements and trade-offs

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the address translation mechanism in contiguous memory allocation. How does the MMU use base and limit registers?
keyPoints: Logical address generated by CPU = (base, offset) or just offset value
MMU (Memory Management Unit) performs translation
Valid address check: offset < limit register, else trap
Physical address = base register + logical address (offset)
Base register: starting physical address of process
Limit register: size of process partition
Example: base=1000, limit=500, logical address 250 → physical=1250
If logical address 550 (≥500), generates protection fault
Diagram shows: CPU → logical addr → MMU → +base → physical addr → Memory
